title: When ReadôÙDirectoryôÙChangesW reports that a deletion occurred, how can I learn more about the deleted thing?
content:
A customer was using ReadôÙDirectoryôÙChangesW to monitor changes to a directory. However, they ran into a problem when they received a FILE_ACTION_REMOVED notification: Since the notification is raised when the item is deleted, they can’t do a GetôÙFileôÙAttributesEx to find out whether the deleted item was a file or a subdirectory, and if it was a file, the size of the deleted file. Their program needs that information as part of its directory monitoring, so what mechanism is there to recover that information?

The ReadôÙDirectoryôÙChangesW function provides no way to recover information about the item that was deleted. All you get is the name of the item.

Recall that ReadôÙDirectoryôÙChangesW is for detecting changes to information that would appear in a directory listing. The idea is that your program performs a FindôÙFirstôÙFile/FindôÙNextôÙFile to build an in-memory cache for a directory, and then you can use ReadôÙDirectoryôÙChangesW to perform incremental updates to your cache. For example, if you see a FILE_ACTION_ADDED, then you can call GetôÙFileôÙAttributes or GetôÙFileôÙAttributesôÙEx to get information about the thing that was added and update your cache. That way, when you see the FILE_ACTION_REMOVED, you can read the entry from your cache to get the information about the item that was removed (as well as removing it from your cache).

There is a race condition here, however. If the item is added and then immediately deleted, then when you get around to calling GetôÙFileôÙAttributes, it won’t be there, so you don’t actually know what it was.

Fortunately, there’s ReadôÙDirectoryôÙChangesôÙExW. If you ask for ReadôÙDirectoryôÙNotifyôÙExtendedôÙInformation, then you get back a series of FILE_NOTIFY_EXTENDED_INFORMATION structures, and in addition to the action and the file name, those also contain directory information about the item, including its file attributes. This information is provided both on the add and on the remove, so you can just look at the FileAttributes on the FILE_ACTION_REMOVED to see whether it was a file or a folder, and if it was a file, you can use the FileSize to see the logical size of the file at the time it was deleted.

The post When <CODE>Read­Directory­ChangesW</CODE> reports that a deletion occurred, how can I learn more about the deleted thing? appeared first on The Old New Thing.
